content: src/index.ts must contain a top-level `const VERSION = "x.y.z";` literal — the publish workflow (.github/workflows/publish.yml) grep-extracts this line and aborts publish if it disagrees with package.json. Do not refactor VERSION into a function call, computed expression, or different file without updating the workflow regex (`grep -oP 'const VERSION = "\K[^"]+' src/index.ts`).

Package name is `@os-eco/warren-cli` (not `@os-eco/warren` as suggested in SPEC §1, which marks it TBD). The publish workflow npm-views `@os-eco/warren-cli` to gate publishing — keep this name in package.json or the workflow short-circuits. Sibling tools follow the same `*-cli` pattern (`@os-eco/seeds-cli`, `@os-eco/burrow-cli`).

src/ subdirectory tree (core, registry, projects, runs, burrow-client, supervisor, server, db, cli, ui) is laid out by SPEC §7 but materialised lazily — each phase (warren-35db onward) creates the files it needs. Phase 0 only ships src/index.ts (VERSION) + src/cli/main.ts (commander wiring) + src/index.test.ts (smoke test). Do not pre-stub empty modules; let later phases own their files.

name: Quality gates triplet
description: Quality gates are `bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck` (the same triplet CONTRIBUTING.md and .github/workflows/ci.yml require). Biome config is tab indent + 100-col, with `noUnusedVariables/Imports/FunctionParameters` and `noExplicitAny` as errors — same posture as @os-eco/seeds. Run all three before committing; CI will reject on any failure.

name: Sibling repo configs
description: When scaffolding new pieces (db config, server skeleton, CLI command shape), reference `/Users/jayminwest/Projects/os-eco/burrow` and `/Users/jayminwest/Projects/os-eco/seeds` first — they pin the os-eco-wide conventions warren follows: tsconfig (strict + noUncheckedIndexedAccess + allowImportingTsExtensions), biome (tab + 100-col), commander CLI shape, drizzle.config.ts pattern. Burrow is the closest in domain (HTTP server + drizzle + supervisor) and the most useful template.

title: Wire @os-eco/burrow-cli via file:../burrow until burrow is published
rationale: Burrow's HttpClient (lib/http-client.ts) is warren's only seam to the runtime, but @os-eco/burrow-cli isn't on npm yet. file:../burrow makes the sibling repo install resolvable in this monorepo layout (bun install creates a symlink-ish copy under node_modules/@os-eco/burrow-cli). Flip to a semver range when burrow ships — warren-345f tracks. Don't inline-copy burrow's wire types; they need to stay in lockstep with burrow's server responses.

description: TypeScript test stubs for typeof fetch fail strict typecheck because fetch's signature requires a preconnect() method that bare async functions lack (Property 'preconnect' is missing in type ...). Resolution: cast via a small helper, e.g. function stub(impl): typeof fetch { return impl as unknown as typeof fetch; }. Don't try to satisfy the full Function-with-preconnect shape — preconnect is never invoked by fetch consumers in tests.
resolution: Use a stub() helper in test files that does 'return impl as unknown as typeof fetch'. Lint allows it; tsc passes; runtime is unaffected because consumer code never calls fetch.preconnect.

description: Bun's biome formatter rewrites multi-line throw-with-options-object into a single line up to lineWidth=100, but only after the lint rule fires. Long error messages with template literals + recoveryHint object commonly fail format check on first write.
resolution: Run 'bun run lint:fix' before committing. The auto-fix is mechanical and safe; the rewrite is purely cosmetic.

content: UI build entrypoint from repo root: 'bun run build:ui' shells 'cd src/ui && bun install && bun run build'. Output lands at src/ui/dist/{index.html, assets/*}. Server picks it up via WARREN_UI_DIST_DIR (defaults to <cwd>/src/ui/dist via src/server/config.ts:resolveUiDistDir). UI handler (src/server/ui.ts) refuses /agents, /projects, /runs, /healthz, /readyz prefixes and falls through to index.html for any other GET so the SPA can render deep links.

Container ENTRYPOINT is 'bun run src/supervisor/main.ts', NOT warren or burrow directly. The supervisor (src/supervisor/main.ts) owns boot ordering (spawn burrow, wait for socket, then spawn warren), signal forwarding (SIGTERM/SIGINT to both children with 5s grace), and burrow restart policy (5-in-60s budget). Both Dockerfile and fly.toml reference the supervisor as the entrypoint.

title: Two-stage Dockerfile: ui-builder + runtime-from-burrow-base
rationale: Stage 1 (oven/bun:1.1 AS ui-builder) builds the React/Vite SPA and emits dist/. Stage 2 extends ghcr.io/jayminwest/burrow-base:0.2.0 (which provides bun + bwrap + uidmap + burrow CLI), installs the four os-eco CLIs warren shells out to (canopy/seeds/mulch/sapling), copies warren source, then COPYs the prebuilt UI bundle from stage 1. This avoids shipping node_modules for the UI in the final image and avoids running 'bun run build:ui' inside the runtime stage where the Vite toolchain isn't otherwise needed.

content: docker-compose.yml applies the four bwrap-friendly security flags (apparmor=unconfined, seccomp=unconfined, systempaths=unconfined, cap_add: SYS_ADMIN) to the warren service. fly.toml does NOT set these — Fly Machines are Firecracker VMs, not containers, and bwrap nests on a stock Machine kernel. Same image, different container posture per orchestrator. See burrow/DEPLOY.md for the underlying recipe (mx-94901b/mx-c085ba).

description: Biome's lint/style/noNonNullAssertion auto-fix (lint:fix) replaces 'arr[idx]!' with 'arr[idx]?', silently turning a compile-time guarantee into a runtime no-op. With noUncheckedIndexedAccess in tsconfig, indexed access is T|undefined; '!' was the assertion the array slot is populated, '?' makes the call (resolveExit, etc.) silently no-op when the slot is empty — masking test bugs.
resolution: Refactor the test to a for-loop that explicitly throws on undefined: 'for (let i...) { const child = arr[i]; if (child === undefined) throw new Error(...); child.method(); }'. Don't accept the auto-fix.

title: Warren is not published to npm; container image is the only distribution channel
rationale: Warren's CLI surface (serve, register-agent, add-project, run, doctor) is server-internal — every command operates on warren's local sqlite and the burrow unix socket inside the deployed container, not on an operator's laptop. Cross-checked the os-eco tree: nothing imports @os-eco/warren-cli or shells out to 'warren'/'wr' (unlike burrow, which overstory/sapling/the warren Dockerfile itself all install globally). The Dockerfile also bakes warren via COPY of source rather than 'bun install -g @os-eco/warren-cli', so even warren's own deploy did not consume itself from npm. The container image (Fly Machine / docker-compose, per SPEC §10.2/10.3) is the canonical artifact. Maintaining the publish flow (NPM_TOKEN secret, version-sync check against src/index.ts, CHANGELOG discipline) was overhead with zero consumers, and a published @os-eco/warren-cli would have invited users to 'npm i -g' it and hit confusing failures running 'wr serve' outside the container.

description: When warren's supervisor calls Bun.spawn({ cmd: ['burrow', ...], cwd: '/app' }), Bun resolves 'burrow' via /app/node_modules/.bin/burrow BEFORE walking PATH. So a Dockerfile that 'bun install -g' a newer @os-eco/burrow-cli to /usr/local/bin/burrow has zero effect on the supervisor's spawned process — it runs the lockfile-pinned local copy. Bumping a CLI that warren both depends on (library) AND ships globally requires updating BOTH the Dockerfile pin AND package.json + bun.lock; otherwise the supervisor runs the stale local bin.
resolution: Bump @os-eco/burrow-cli (and any other dual-purpose CLI) in three places: Dockerfile global install line, package.json deps, and bun.lock (regenerate via 'bun install'). Verify post-rebuild: docker compose exec warren sh -c 'for pid in /proc/[0-9]*; do tr "\\0" " " < "$pid/cmdline"; done | grep burrow.serve' — if the path is /app/node_modules/.bin/burrow, the lockfile pin is what's running.

name: claude-code-postinstall-not-run-by-bun-install-g
description: @anthropic-ai/claude-code declares a postinstall script that downloads the platform-native 'claude' binary. Bun's 'bun install -g' skips lifecycle scripts by default for safety, so the bin shim at /usr/local/bin/claude just prints 'Error: claude native binary not installed' and exits 1. Workaround in Dockerfile: after 'bun install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@<v>', run 'bun run /root/.bun/install/global/node_modules/@anthropic-ai/claude-code/install.cjs' to invoke the postinstall manually. Same pattern likely applies to any npm package that uses postinstall to fetch native binaries.

biome's noFallthroughSwitchClause flags a switch case whose body ends in process.exit() — biome only treats break/return/throw/continue as terminators, not Node's never-returning calls. CI lint failed on scripts/acceptance/run.ts where 'case "-h": printHelp(); process.exit(0);' fell through to default.
resolution: Add an explicit 'break;' after process.exit(0) (it's unreachable but satisfies the rule). Same pattern applies to any switch-case body terminating in process.exit / never-returning helpers.

content: release workflow (.github/workflows/release.yml) tags + creates GH release on every push to main where package.json version doesn't yet exist as v$VERSION on origin tags. To cut a new release: bump package.json + src/index.ts VERSION (must match — workflow verifies), add a CHANGELOG.md '## [X.Y.Z]' section, push to main. No npm publish (warren ships as container per mx-834a3a). v0.1.0 was tagged manually before the workflow landed.

description: bun-types' Serve.Options is XOR(HostnamePortServeOptions, UnixServeOptions); idleTimeout is declared only on the TCP variant, so Bun.serve({ unix, fetch, idleTimeout }) fails typecheck with 'Type number is not assignable to type undefined'. Bun's runtime DOES honor idleTimeout on unix sockets — the type is the gap.
resolution: Cast through unknown when binding: const opts = { unix, fetch, idleTimeout } as unknown as Parameters<typeof Bun.serve>[0]; return Bun.serve(opts);

content: Dockerfile must symlink /app/src/cli/main.ts → /usr/local/bin/warren AND /usr/local/bin/wr after the COPY . /app step. The five os-eco CLIs are installed via 'bun install -g' but warren itself is not, so its package.json bin entries (warren, wr) only land on PATH via an explicit symlink. main.ts is +x with a '#!/usr/bin/env bun' shebang so the symlink runs as-is. Without it 'docker compose exec warren warren <subcmd>' fails with 'executable file not found in PATH' and operators must fall back to 'bun /app/src/cli/main.ts'.

Runtime image installs curl alongside bubblewrap/uidmap/git/ca-certificates so first-run diagnostics against the burrow unix socket (curl --unix-socket /var/run/burrow.sock http://localhost/healthz) work out of the box — no bun -e fetch() workarounds. ~1MB image cost; debugging time saved is hours. Don't drop curl when slimming the image without first wiring an alternative diagnostic path.

description: drizzle-kit generate (v0.31.10) for SQLite emits CREATE INDEX with the descending expression wrapped in backticks: CREATE INDEX foo ON runs (`project_id`,`"started_at" DESC`). SQLite then treats "started_at" DESC as a single literal column name and migrate fails at runtime with 'no such column: "started_at" DESC'. The original 0000_init.sql (older drizzle-kit) used the correct form (project_id,"started_at" DESC) — no backticks around the expression.
resolution: After 'bunx drizzle-kit generate', hand-edit the new migration .sql to drop the backticks around any indexed expression like `"col" DESC` → "col" DESC. The bun-sqlite migrator runs migrations on every openDatabase call, so a bad index expression surfaces immediately in any unit test that opens an in-memory db.

description: Warren's Dockerfile global bun install lands in /root/.bun/install/global by default, but burrow's sandbox SYSTEM_RO_MOUNTS only ro-binds /usr, /etc, /lib, /lib64, /bin, /sbin, /opt — so /usr/local/bin/{sd,ml,cn,sapling,burrow} symlinks dangle inside the sandbox and the agent can't run any os-eco CLI.
resolution: Set ENV BUN_INSTALL=/usr/local before 'bun install -g'. Bun then stores packages under /usr/local/install/global/node_modules/, which sits under the /usr ro-bind, so symlinks resolve. Update the explicit claude-code postinstall path to match (/usr/local/install/global/node_modules/@anthropic-ai/claude-code/install.cjs).

title: Acceptance harness wraps burrow serve with a custom entry that registers stub-shell + uses no-bwrap spawn
rationale: Two upstream gaps blocked acceptance scenarios that need a real running agent: (1) burrow serve's AgentRegistry only carries built-ins; declarative [[agents]] in burrow.toml are read for toolchain mounts but never registered into the runtime registry (node_modules/@os-eco/burrow-cli/src/runtime/registry.ts comment is aspirational). (2) burrow's runSandboxed needs CAP_SYS_ADMIN to mount /proc in a fresh userns; ordinary dev/CI containers without the bwrap-friendly compose flags fail with 'Operation not permitted'. The wrapper at scripts/acceptance/lib/burrow-with-stub.ts imports Client.open() and runServeCommand from @os-eco/burrow-cli, programmatically registers the stub-shell AgentConfig via loadAgentConfig + client.agents.register, and passes a no-bwrap SpawnFn (direct Bun.spawn against the workspace dir) through dispatcherOptions.spawn. inproc.ts's spawnBurrow then launches 'bun run burrow-with-stub.ts' instead of 'burrow serve'. Production warren still runs 'burrow serve' via the supervisor — the wrapper is acceptance-only.

content: Acceptance container mode (scripts/acceptance/lib/compose.ts) does NOT bind-mount the host canopy/sample fixtures into the container — the production deploy is fixture-free, so the harness mirrors that shape. Scenarios that need fixtures or that drive process control (kill/restart) declare modes:['in-proc']; container mode runs only scenarios 01 (boot/healthz/readyz) and 13 (container smoke). Override yaml in tmp inlines WARREN_API_TOKEN, WARREN_BURROW_NO_AUTH=1, blank CANOPY_REPO_URL; never reads .env. Each run uses a unique compose project name (warren-acceptance-<random>) so parallel runs don't collide, and a random ephemeral host port mapped to :8080. Boot timeout is 120s (first run builds the image); teardown is 'docker compose down -v --timeout 5'.

package.json exposes 'acceptance' (in-proc default) and 'acceptance:container' (compose-based). ACCEPTANCE.md at repo root is the operator runbook for V1 — covers the trinity (bun test/lint/typecheck), in-proc + container modes, and the three manual gates that the automated harness deliberately doesn't try to cover (--real claude-code run, fly deploy, UI smoke). Manual gates exist because token cost / cloud time / visual inspection can't be cheaply automated; SPEC §11.E–§11.G post-mortems are the canonical list of failure shapes the manual gates catch (warren-67cc, warren-a69a, warren-1eaa, warren-1a09, branchPushed-requires-both-reap-and-sandbox-git).

title: warren-config formats: triggers.yaml + defaults.json (per pl-5d74 step 1)
rationale: ROADMAP R-02 locks YAML for triggers (cron expressions read better than TOML/JSON, arrays-of-objects are clean) and the plan keeps defaults as JSON for symmetry with the rest of os-eco's UI/API JSON surface and to avoid forcing a YAML round-trip on the simpler defaults file. Note: ROADMAP R-02 originally proposed defaults.yaml; pl-5d74 deviates to defaults.json — captured here so future readers see one source of truth before the ROADMAP edit lands in step 8 (warren-8107).

title: YAML parser: js-yaml ^4.1.1 (matches mulch + overstory)
rationale: pl-5d74 risk #2 calls for using the same YAML lib as siblings to avoid ecosystem drift. mulch (^4.1.1 in deps + ^4.0.9 @types/js-yaml in devDeps) and overstory (@types/js-yaml ^4.0.9 in devDeps) both standardize on js-yaml; seeds/canopy/sapling/greenhouse/burrow have no YAML dep. Picked js-yaml@^4.1.1 + @types/js-yaml@^4.0.9 to mirror mulch's pin exactly. Considered the 'yaml' package — equally maintained but would introduce a second YAML lib into the os-eco install graph.

title: Cron lib: croner ^10.0.1 for R-06 scheduler
rationale: pl-2f15 step 1 picks the cron parser for the R-06 scheduler. Plan approach evaluated four alternatives and selected croner because: (1) small (~30 KB), no native deps, no postinstall scripts — fits warren's tiny-dep posture; (2) tz-aware with explicit DST handling, which a homegrown 5-token parser would skip and later regret (plan risk #2); (3) actively maintained, MIT-licensed, single file; (4) supports both 5-token (Vixie) and 6-token (seconds) grammars, which lets warren-config keep its loose 'cron expression validation' (mx-40fe51) and defer strict parsing to fire time. Bring-our-own parser was rejected — cron grammar corner cases (step values, ranges, DOW vs DOM, 6-token seconds) would either be wrong or grow into croner anyway.

content: Adding a drizzle migration: edit schema.ts → bun run db:generate emits src/db/migrations/NNNN_<random-codename>.sql + meta/NNNN_snapshot.json + appends to _journal.json. Then (1) rename the SQL to a descriptive NNNN_<verb_noun>.sql, (2) update the matching 'tag' in meta/_journal.json so the migrator can find it, (3) run 'bunx biome format --write' on the new snapshot + journal — drizzle-kit emits 2-space indent but biome.json enforces tabs.

When R-06 (cron scheduler) ships new trigger kinds or new TriggerSchema fields, update three places in lockstep: ROADMAP R-06 entry, SPEC §11.I (and §11.H if the warren-config schema changes), and acceptance scenario 15 (scripts/acceptance/scenarios/15-triggers-roundtrip.ts). New TriggerSchema fields must stay additive (all-optional) so existing triggers.yaml files keep parsing — warren-config (R-02) and the dispatcher (R-06) co-own the schema. Sibling lockstep convention to mx-5339d5 (R-02).

User-facing positioning (README, SPEC §1/§2, package.json description, CLAUDE.md opener, Dockerfile/fly.toml comments) leads with what warren *does* — spawn sandboxed agents at GitHub repos, watch live, steer, get a branch — NOT with the four-tool composition. The os-eco data-plane tools (canopy/mulch/seeds/sapling) are 'opt-in built-in features' that ship in every image but are not required for a fresh install: claude-code is inline, canopy activates on CANOPY_REPO_URL, mulch/seeds activate on per-project .mulch/.seeds/ directories, sapling is just another inline agent. When updating docs that name-drop the four tools as prerequisites, demote them to a 'Power features' section (warren-e576).

.env.example documents multi-provider keys (OPENAI_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY, GOOGLE_API_KEY, PI_API_KEY, GROQ_API_KEY, MISTRAL_API_KEY, DEEPSEEK_API_KEY) commented out by default (warren-f8c0). The supervisor inherits container env into both warren and burrow children via Bun.spawn without an explicit env: {} — no docker-compose.yml change needed. Burrow's piRuntime.envPassthrough is what actually forwards the keys into the sandbox; warren just makes sure they reach the burrow process.

Drizzle-generated migrations land with a random name (e.g. 0006_dusty_jimmy_woo.sql). Rename to the project convention (0006_add_run_cost_columns.sql) and update the matching tag in src/db/migrations/meta/_journal.json in the same change — drizzle reads the tag from the journal, not the filename. The companion meta/NNNN_snapshot.json stays as-is (keyed by idx, not name).

'bun run lint' (biome check --error-on-warnings .) descends into .claude/worktrees/<branch>/ and fails with 'Found a nested root configuration' when agent worktrees exist locally — each worktree has its own biome.json. Workaround: run 'bunx biome check --error-on-warnings src/ scripts/ drizzle.config.ts' to scan explicit paths and bypass the nested-root detection. Pre-existing local-environment condition; biome.json on main is unchanged.

Bumping @os-eco/burrow-cli to pick up a new built-in runtime (e.g. piRuntime in 0.2.8) is a pin-only change in three lockstep locations (package.json + bun.lock + Dockerfile; per CLAUDE.md 'Relationship to burrow'). No warren source changes needed — burrow's BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES is loaded by the supervisor's burrow serve. The acceptance harness (scripts/acceptance/lib/burrow-with-stub.ts) keeps its declarative stub registration for the new agent because the agent binary (e.g. pi) isn't installed in CI/dev; production warren talks to burrow's real runtime. Quality gates after bump: bun install → bun test + tsc + biome.

description: Bun-based docker images (oven/bun:1.2) provide a node-compat shim at /usr/local/bun-node-fallback-bin/node, but that directory is NOT on the PATH burrow exposes inside bwrap (only /usr/local/bin is bind-mounted). CLIs with #!/usr/bin/env node shebangs (like @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent) fail immediately inside the sandbox with 'env: node: No such file or directory'.
resolution: Add a Dockerfile symlink before WORKDIR: ln -s /usr/local/bun-node-fallback-bin/node /usr/local/bin/node. Confirmed working for pi v0.74.0; bun's shim handles its node-script semantics fine. Same approach generalizes to any node-shebanged CLI installed via 'bun install -g'.

content: Warren version bump for the R-07 disk-seam closure landed as 0.2.0 (warren plan pl-a31c, parent warren-0a83) — the minor bump signals that warren↔burrow is now HTTP-only on every code path except reap's mulch_merge + branch_push. package.json + src/index.ts must move in lockstep (release.yml grep-checks); CHANGELOG.md 0.2.0 entry references burrow plan bur-pl-2467 as the burrow-side R-07 origin.

WARREN_RUN_BRANCH_PREFIX (warren-9993) env loader lives in src/runs/branch.ts loadRunBranchPrefixFromEnv — same lenient shape as loadAutoOpenPrConfigFromEnv (src/runs/pr.ts): unset/whitespace/invalid → undefined, never throws. Wired in three places: src/server/main.ts bootServer reads once and threads via ServerDeps.runBranchPrefixDefault and BootSchedulerInput.runBranchPrefixDefault; src/cli/commands/run.ts defaults deps.runBranchPrefixDefault to a fresh loadRunBranchPrefixFromEnv() call (passing null forces the built-in 'burrow' for tests). Documented in .env.example below the WARREN_AUTO_OPEN_PR block.

name: acceptance pi declarative outputFormat gap
description: The acceptance harness registers pi as a declarative agent (scripts/acceptance/lib/burrow-with-stub.ts PI_AGENT_CONFIG with outputFormat:'raw-text'). Burrow's declarative agent only supports raw-text / stream-json / jsonl-claude (burrow src/runtime/declarative.ts:130-134) — there is no jsonl-pi outputFormat. So even after warren-17a4's in-stream cost extraction landed, scenario 16 (16-pi-parity-smoke.ts) still can't assert non-null cost_usd / tokens_* — burrow wraps each pi-shaped JSONL line as {kind:'text', stream:'stdout'} and the bridge's turn_end predicate never matches. Three unblock paths: (1) add jsonl-pi as a declarative outputFormat upstream, (2) install pi binary in CI + switch harness to real piRuntime, (3) register a built-in-style wrapper using parsePiEvents directly.

content: Drizzle migration meta files (src/db/migrations/meta/NNNN_snapshot.json, _journal.json) emitted by drizzle-kit generate are indented with two spaces by default, but biome.json formats them with tabs. After 'bun run db:generate', run 'bun run biome format --write src/db/migrations/meta/NNNN_snapshot.json src/db/migrations/meta/_journal.json' or 'bun run lint' will fail. Also format the generated 0008_snapshot.json the same way. (warren-135b)

name: Bun.TOML.parse is built-in
description: Bun ships native TOML parsing as Bun.TOML.parse(string) — throws on malformed input, returns plain JS object. No external TOML dependency needed in warren (used by src/server-config/load.ts for warren.toml). Empty/whitespace/comments-only input returns {}. Confirmed against bun 1.3.6.

content: warren's named volume 'warren_data' (declared in docker-compose.yml) lands on the host as 'warren_warren_data' because docker compose prefixes named volumes with the project name. When running one-shot containers against the data volume (sqlite surgery, manual db inspection), mount warren_warren_data. Verify with 'docker inspect warren --format {{json .Mounts}}' first. Mounting the unprefixed name silently creates a fresh empty volume — symptom is /data appearing empty when warren clearly populated it.
